assessment_items.question_content:
**The term "shibboleth" refers to a kind of  $\_\_\_\_\_\_$**.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The term "shibboleth" refers to a kind of codeword used to distinguish people.


**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1960---age-of-post-colonialism/Art-After-1980/a/doris-salcedo-shibboleth)**

choices.content: a codeword used to distinguish people
a deep fissure in the earth
a 1985 confrontation between rebels and the military in Bogota, Columbia
a scar left on the body after a deep cut heals

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why might *Shibboleth* be described as an example of conceptual art?** 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4d8f1d0e37c989ae3fb34acbd3f3ec50.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In conceptual art,  the ideas that frame the artist's work are of equal, if not greater importance than the material work itself.

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1960---age-of-post-colonialism/Art-After-1980/a/doris-salcedo-shibboleth)**

choices.content: The influence of earlier conceptual artists is apparent.
The ideas that frame the work are as important as the physical object.
The installation requires viewers to interact physically with the entire space.
The artist describes herself as a conceptual artist.
The installation was temporary and exists now only in photographs.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How does *Shibboleth* reflect the influence of earlier artists such as Lucio Fontana (top) and Gordon Matta-Clark (bottom)?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4de45008feb5f18cf3af0ae094bea63b.jpg)

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f56d6f6b4b9e9d4f97e112c80d1a4d60.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Fontana rejected the flat picture plane by slashing the monochromatic surface of his canvases.

Like Fontana and Matta-Clark, Salcedo flaunts subversion by emphasizing the negative space made by the incision to the material.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1960---age-of-post-colonialism/Art-After-1980/a/doris-salcedo-shibboleth)**

choices.content: Its subversive emphasis on negative space.
Its themes of immigration and disenfranchisement.
Its artistic value depends on installation in a museum context.
Its emphasis on the flat picture plane.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which interpretation of *Shibboleth* is best supported by this visual detail?**

 


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/232836ff0faa7ef724be9f61c676eaf3.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The scarred floor in the Turbine Hall left after *Shibboleth* is a remarkable symbol of the possibility of healing through figurative and literal closure; however, the mark is also an obstacle to any attempts to erase the past.

As she walks, the viewer’s perception into the crevice alters to see better inside the cracks and appreciate the interior space, notably the wire mesh embedded along the sides.  Change in perspective is one of Salcedo’s goals. She quotes the Frankfort School theorist Theodor Adorno: “We should all see the world from the perspective of the victim, like Jewish people that were killed with their head down in the Middle Ages. So he wonders, what is the perspective of a person that is agonizing in this position?”

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1960---age-of-post-colonialism/Art-After-1980/a/doris-salcedo-shibboleth)**

choices.content: Racial segregation in Europe marks people from the third-world as permanently set apart.
We should all see the world from the perspective of the victim.
The past should never be completely erased.
Friends and enemies are separated by fine linguistic lines.
